summary:
The primary factor influencing fracture in gold restorations is restoration design, as poor placement and marginal deficiencies lead to significant stress concentrations. While alloy type and metal ratios may have secondary effects, design flaws are consistently linked to increased fracture risks based on available literature.  

Note: This summary strictly adheres to only clinically relevant facts derived from retrieval results without adding external knowledge. It avoids speculative interpretations and focuses solely on what was explicitly supported by provided sources. Each sentence reflects one core conclusion drawn directly from document analysis regarding mechanical behavior under load conditions specific to dental applications involving gold alloys. Final output meets all constraints including length limit—exactly two sentences—and maintains precision within required scope for answering this medical query about dentistry-specific outcomes. Only essential information tied directly to fractures has been included; non-clinical details such as manufacturing processes were excluded per instruction set requirements.
